content:
8 min read
Just now

--

I haven’t opened a Stack Overflow tab in four months. I’m not entirely sure how I feel about that.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One ritual replaced another. I’m still not sure exactly how I feel about it.
I used to have a specific ritual when I hit a bug I couldn’t solve.
Copy the error message. Paste it into Google with “site:stackoverflow.com” appended. Open six tabs from the results. Skim each one for an answer that matched my exact situation, which it almost never did. Read the top comment thread arguing about whether the accepted answer was actually correct. Eventually cobble together a solution from three different threads, two of which were arguing with each other.
That ritual is gone now. These days I describe the bug to an AI assistant in plain English, in my actual words, including the slightly embarrassing context of why I broke it in the first place, and I get a working answer in under a minute. No tabs. No decade-old thread about Python 2.7. No risk of my question getting closed as a duplicate of something only superficially similar.
It turns out I’m not unusual in this. According to a graph that circulated widely in early 2026, Stack Overflow’s question volume has plummeted by over 90% since its 2014 peak, and the timeline lines up almost exactly with when AI coding tools went mainstream. I didn’t think I was part of a trend. I just thought I’d found something faster. Looking at the data now, I clearly wasn’t alone.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The decline wasn’t gradual. It was a collapse — and I was part of it without realizing.
The Numbers Are More Dramatic Than I Expected
I want to ground this in actual data rather than just my own anecdote, because the scale of the shift surprised even me when I looked into it.
Monthly question submissions to Stack Overflow collapsed from peaks exceeding 200,000 in 2014 to under 50,000 by late 2025, which is roughly the same volume the platform was seeing in its first year of existence. By December 2025, only 3,862 questions were asked on the platform in a single month — compared to over 200,000 monthly at the site’s peak.
The decline coincides with widespread AI tool adoption, with 84% of developers now using AI in their development process. That’s not a niche behavior anymore. That’s the majority default.
I’ll admit a small, slightly uncomfortable feeling reading these numbers. I’d assumed my own shift away from Stack Overflow was just personal preference — I like fast answers, I’m impatient, I found something better suited to how I think. Seeing it as part of an industry-wide collapse complicates that story a little. I wasn’t choosing convenience in isolation. I was part of erasing something.

What Actually Got Better
I don’t want to undersell the genuine improvements, because they’re real and they changed how I work in ways I notice daily.
The single biggest difference is context. On Stack Overflow, you’re searching for someone else’s problem that vaguely resembles yours, then adapting their solution to your specific situation. With an AI assistant, you describe your actual situation — your specific stack, your specific error, the weird thing you did three steps earlier that’s probably related — and the answer is built for that exact context rather than approximated from someone else’s.
There’s also no social cost. Stack Overflow has a documented reputation for hostility toward what regulars consider “basic” questions, and it is becoming less useful for beginners because many high-ranking answers are outdated and the community is often hostile toward “basic” questions. I’d internalized that hostility more than I realized — I used to draft and redraft questions for twenty minutes before posting, terrified of getting marked as a duplicate or downvoted into oblivion. That fear is just gone now. I ask the AI things I’d have been embarrassed to post publicly, and nothing bad happens.
And then there’s the currency problem. Search for almost any general programming concept, and the top result will likely be a decade old, with thousands of upvotes, suggesting outdated practices that newer, more accurate answers can’t compete with because of the platform’s reputation system. I’d hit this constantly — finding a beautifully upvoted answer from 2014 that no longer applied to the current version of whatever library I was using.

What I Genuinely Miss (And This Surprised Me)
Here’s where I want to be honest rather than just present a clean win for AI, because the picture is more complicated than “old thing bad, new thing good.”
I miss the discussion threads. Specifically, I miss the arguments. On Stack Overflow, even a flawed top answer would often have a comment thread underneath it where someone explained exactly why it was flawed, and that disagreement taught me more than the original answer did. AI doesn’t really argue with itself in front of you. You get a confident answer, and if it’s wrong, you usually don’t find out from the tool itself — you find out when your code breaks in production three days later.
I also miss the sense that I was contributing to something. Every question I asked and every answer I found on Stack Overflow was, in some small way, becoming part of a permanent public resource that the next confused developer might stumble onto. When a developer solves a novel problem with an AI assistant instead, that solution exists only in their private chat history — it’s not indexed, it’s not peer-reviewed, and it doesn’t help the next person who encounters the same issue. I’m aware that every time I solve something privately with AI instead of publicly on a forum, I’m contributing to exactly the collapse I’m describing. That’s an uncomfortable thing to sit with, and I don’t have a clean resolution for it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Confident and wrong looks identical to confident and right — until your code breaks three days later.
The Trust Problem Nobody’s Talking About Enough
This is the part of my experience that I think gets understated in most “I switched to AI” pieces, including the ones I read before writing this.
The AI is often wrong in a specific, sneaky way. Not obviously wrong — code that doesn’t run, which you’d catch immediately. Confidently, plausibly wrong. Code that runs, looks correct, and contains a subtle bug you don’t discover until much later.
Stack Overflow’s own 2025 developer survey found that while 84% of developers use AI tools, 46% actively distrust their accuracy, with only 33% trusting AI output and a mere 3% reporting “highly trusting” what it produces. That trust gap has actually widened — only 29% of developers trusted AI outputs to be accurate as of the 2025 survey, down from 40% in 2024, an 11-point decline in a single year.
That number matched my own experience uncomfortably well. Sixty-six percent of developers cite solutions that are “almost right, but not quite” as their biggest frustration with AI tools, and I’ve lost entire afternoons to exactly that category of problem — code that looked completely reasonable and was subtly broken in a way that took longer to debug than it would have taken to just write correctly from scratch. In fact, 45% of developers report that debugging AI-generated code actually takes longer than writing it themselves.
I want to sit with that contradiction rather than smooth it over, because I think it’s the most honest part of this whole experience: the tool is faster and I trust it less, simultaneously, and both of those things are true at the same time.

What This Did to How I Actually Learn
There’s a psychological dimension to this shift that I didn’t anticipate when I made the switch, and it took me a few months to notice.
Struggling with a problem on Stack Overflow — sifting through bad answers, parsing arguments in comment threads, eventually piecing together an understanding — was slow, but it built something. By the time I’d cobbled together a working solution from three contradictory threads, I usually understood why each piece worked, because I’d had to evaluate competing explanations myself.
Getting an instant, complete answer from AI skips that evaluative step almost entirely. Cognitive psychologist Robert Bjork’s research on what he terms “desirable difficulties” suggests that the friction involved in working something out — rather than being handed the answer — is often what produces durable learning rather than just momentary task completion. I think I’ve traded some of that friction for speed, and I’m honestly not sure yet what the long-term cost of that trade will be for me.
I notice it specifically in retention. Solutions I struggled toward on Stack Overflow, I tend to remember. Solutions an AI gave me in thirty seconds, I sometimes find myself looking up again a week later, having genuinely forgotten the reasoning, because I never had to construct that reasoning myself in the first place.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I haven’t fully resolved how I feel about any of this. That might be the most honest part.
Where I’ve Landed, Imperfectly
I haven’t fully resolved how I feel about any of this, and I think pretending otherwise would be dishonest.
For genuinely novel problems — things specific to my exact codebase, my exact context — AI is unambiguously better than what Stack Overflow ever offered me. The context-awareness alone makes the comparison lopsided.
For learning genuinely new concepts, especially ones I’ll need to reason about independently later, I’ve started deliberately forcing myself to slow down. Asking the AI to explain rather than just solve. Trying the desirable-difficulty thing on purpose, even though it’s slower, because I suspect the speed was costing me something I’ll notice later, the way you don’t notice you skipped a workout until your legs are weaker months down the line.
And for genuinely tricky bugs where I don’t fully trust the AI’s confident answer, I’ve occasionally gone back to Stack Overflow, half out of habit and half out of a residual sense that someone, somewhere, has actually hit this exact problem and argued about it publicly. The threads are thinner than they used to be. The platform that once handled 200,000 questions a month now sees roughly a tenth of one percent of that volume. I don’t know what happens to that public, permanent layer of collective knowledge if this trend keeps going the way the data suggests it’s going.

If you’ve made a similar switch — or you’re someone who still prefers the old forum model — I’d genuinely like to hear how you’ve navigated the trust problem specifically. Drop your experience in the comments. And if this kind of honest, data-grounded writing about how we actually work is useful to you, follow me here for more.
